subject: [zzzzteana] Man admits Soham kidnapping hoax calls
body:
Ananova: 
Man admits Soham kidnapping hoax calls

A man, deeply affected by the tragic disappearance of Soham schoolgirls Jessica Chapman and Holly Wells, has admitted making calls to police in an attempt to help with the investigation. 

Howard Youde, a resident of Wrexham, was concerned about the lack of progress in the case and, in a moment of desperation, made three calls to Cambridgeshire police, believing he might have crucial information.  

He was apprehended in Wrexham when officers traced the calls to a phone box.  Mr. Youde, who has been struggling with personal difficulties, claims he has no recollection of making the calls due to excessive alcohol consumption.

His lawyer, Mark Arden, emphasized that Mr. Youde's actions were driven by a misguided desire to assist the investigation and were not intended to cause any harm or distress. He acknowledged the seriousness of the situation and expressed deep remorse for any unintended consequences.

Mr. Youde has been released on bail pending a pre-sentence report, during which time he will be undergoing counseling to address his alcohol abuse and emotional distress.

subject: [DMDX] write to output file, disk full
body:
Subject: [DMDX] write to output file, disk full

Hi

I'm working on a really cool project using DMDX to analyze facial expressions in response to different stimuli.  I'm trying to create a dataset of anonymized faces for research purposes.

I've run into a strange issue though.  The program seems to be working fine during the syntax check, but when it gets to displaying the first face, I get an error message saying "Write to output file, disk full".

I've double-checked, and the disk definitely isn't full. I've tried adjusting the timing and video settings, and even updated to the latest DMDX version, but nothing seems to work.

Any ideas what could be causing this? I'm really eager to get this project moving!

Thanks,
arch

subject: Re: [Python-3000] lambda
body:
Lennart Regebro wrote:
> On Tue, Mar 25, 2008 at 9:42 PM, Olivier Verdier  wrote:
>> repeats over and over again: lambda is just synctactic sugar for a function
>> definition. Here are suggestion for a new name for lambda, which will much
>> better describe what it is, namely a *function*.
> 
> More specifically, an anonymous function.  Best would be if you could
> just use def anonymously. And preferrably use normal return syntax:
> 
>    def(a,b): return a+b
> 
> instead of
> 
>    lambda a, b: a+b
> 
> would actually make it readable enough that I might start using it. ;)

Nick is suggesting a change to Python's syntax to make it more user-friendly. He believes that using "def" for anonymous functions would improve readability and make the code more self-documenting.  He acknowledges that the current "lambda" syntax is concise but feels that a more explicit approach would be beneficial for beginners and experienced programmers alike.

Cheers,
Nick.

--

subject: [ILUG] One Command -- Four Types
body:
Subject: [ILUG] One Command -- Four Types

I've been working on a project to streamline our system administration tasks and was exploring different ways to optimize the use of the `ln` command.  I noticed there are several variations of hostname links, each with its own unique options: `/bin/dnsdomainname`, `/bin/domainname`, `/bin/nisdomainname`, and `/bin/ypdomainname`.

It seems a bit redundant to have four separate commands for essentially the same purpose.  

Could someone shed some light on the rationale behind this design choice?  I'm curious to understand the specific use cases for each type of link and how they might be best utilized in a modern system environment.

-- 
Note: When you reply to this message, please include the mailing
      list and/or newsgroup address and my email address in To:

*********************************************************************
Signed,
SoloCDM

-- 
Irish Linux Users' Group: ilug@linux.ie
http://www.linux.ie/mailman/listinfo/ilug for (un)subscription information.

subject: PAPER 6
body:
This email discusses the fascinating world of distributed data management and search optimization within peer-to-peer networks. 

It introduces Beehive, a novel system designed to enhance the performance of applications like DNS and web servers. Beehive leverages the inherent scalability of existing P2P networks like Pastry by intelligently predicting and replicating popular data. This proactive approach ensures that users often find the information they need instantly, significantly improving response times.

The email delves into the technical details of Beehive's design, explaining how it dynamically adjusts replication levels based on data popularity and utilizes clever aggregation techniques to gather popularity information efficiently. It also touches upon the importance of versioning for maintaining data consistency and the ongoing research into optimizing Beehive's performance further.

The email then shifts focus to a research paper exploring search strategies within P2P networks. It highlights the limitations of traditional flooding methods and presents innovative solutions like expanding ring search and "checking" – a technique that incorporates random walks to efficiently terminate searches.

Finally, the email discusses the importance of replication strategies in P2P networks, emphasizing the superiority of a square-root replication approach for minimizing average search sizes.


This email provides a valuable overview of cutting-edge research in distributed systems and offers insights into the challenges

subject: Re: [ILUG] extending a HAN with Wireless..
body:
Hello Justin,

I understand you're looking to expand your home network with wireless capabilities.  That's great!  It's a fantastic way to add flexibility and convenience.

Connecting a wireless access point to your hub is a simple and effective solution.  You can then use a wireless PCMCIA network card in your laptop and PCMCIA to PCI adapters or wireless PCI network cards for your desktop machines.  

The access point will need a network name (SSID) and channel number, which you'll need to configure on your client machines as well.  

Don't worry about driver support, it's very good for wireless hardware.  Most older 802.11b and 802.11a hardware has Linux drivers, so you should be all set.

If you run into any issues, the forums and IRC channel at [link removed] are great resources for help and information.

Good luck with your wireless setup!

Robert Fitzsimons

subject: RE: Hanson's Sept 11 message in the National Review
body:
Subject: RE: Hanson's Sept 11 message in the National Review

On Thu, 19 Sep 2002, Bill Stoddard wrote:
--]How likely are you to change someone's mind by being
--]rude and disrespectful to them? Is this how to win friends and influence
--]people?

Point the first, I think it's important to understand the motivations behind these strong reactions.  It's possible they aren't trying to change Rumsy's mind directly, but rather to spark a wider discussion and debate on the topic.  By making their views known so forcefully, they're hoping to bring more attention to the issue and encourage others to engage in thoughtful conversation.

Point the second. Throughout history, passionate and outspoken individuals have often played a crucial role in driving social change. Think of the bold and sometimes confrontational figures who fought for civil rights or challenged unjust laws. Their willingness to speak out, even if it was unpopular, helped to raise awareness and ultimately led to positive change.

Now, point the third, I understand that this kind of direct approach can be unsettling.  But sometimes, a little discomfort is necessary to break through complacency and encourage people to think critically about important issues

subject: Re: Porting the expansion libraries
body:
Well, I've been working on a project using an expansion board and have made some progress porting the libraries. I've successfully got the digital_out.o, servo.o, and gp2d02.o modules working. They're still in a testing phase, but they're showing a lot of promise.

I took a look at the existing code from Fred Martin and Barry Brouillette, and was able to adapt it quite easily for my needs.  I'm not personally using the LEGO and analog input features, but I'm sure they could be incorporated with minimal effort.

If anyone else is interested in exploring these libraries or wants to help with testing, please let me know. I'm happy to share my progress and collaborate. 


I was inspired by Chris Prosser's mention of ports for gcc and Imagecraft C.  It's great to see the community working together to make these libraries accessible to everyone.  

I'm also impressed by Curt Mills's work on the HC11 GCC port. It's amazing what can be achieved with open-source collaboration!

subject: [DMDX]
body:
Dear List Members,

I'm working on a fascinating project exploring how people process information in a self-paced manner. I'm using DMDX to create a word-by-word experiment where participants can control the speed at which they read a text passage.

I've been experimenting with different methods to achieve this, including using empty frames to create pauses between words. However, I'm finding that even with these pauses, the pace is still too fast for my needs.

I'm particularly interested in implementing a "ResponseContingent" mode, which would allow participants to decide when to move to the next word. However, the DMDX documentation warns against using this mode.

My question is: are there any alternative methods within DMDX that would allow me to achieve a similar effect, where participants can control the pace of the experiment without relying on the potentially problematic "ResponseContingent" mode?

Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

Best regards,

Irina Sekerina, Ph.D.
Rutgers University Center for Cognitive Science
http://ruccs.rutgers.edu/~sekerina

subject: [opensuse] SAN device
body:
Subject: [opensuse] SAN device

Greetings,

We're running opensuse 10.2 and are really excited about expanding our virtualization capabilities! We use this server as a host for a couple of vmware guest servers. 

As part of our upgrade to a more robust infrastructure, we've recently integrated this server with our SAN. The attached SAN LUN is showing up in the Yast partitioner as an EVMS drive (/dev/evms/sdb).  I'm a bit puzzled because I was expecting it to appear as /dev/sdb.

Could you please clarify if EVMS is the standard way to connect to a SAN in opensuse?  We want to make sure we're using the best practices for our new setup.

Thanks for your expertise!

Michael Edwards
Farmer school of business

Emma

subject: [Razor-users] Fire.cloudmark.com is having issues
body:
Hi everyone,

I've been working on optimizing our spam filtering system and noticed something interesting.  It seems like the order in which we process different spam filters can affect the results.  

For example, when I prioritize "truth" in our filter catalog, we get a positive spam detection. However, if I move "fire.cloudmark.com" higher in the list, the positive detection disappears.  

This might indicate a potential issue with how our system is communicating with Hubris, where our reports are sent. I'm going to investigate further and see if there's a way to improve the synchronization between these two systems.

Best,

Bobby

subject: Re: [UAI] Imprecise Probabilities--A simple and yet computationally nontrivial problem
body:
Lotfi Zadeh wrote:
>          X and Y are random variables taking values in the set (1, 2, ...,n). The entries in the joint probability matrix, P, are of the form "approximately aij," where the aij take values in the unit interval and add up to unity. What is the marginal probability distribution of X? Four special cases: (a) "approximately aij," is interpreted as an interval centering on aij; (b) "approximately aij," is interpreted as a triangular fuzzy number centering on aij; (c) "approximately aij," is interpreted as a uniform probability distribution over an interval centering on aij; and (d) "approximatelt aij," is interpreted as a triangular probability density function centering on aij.

Let me explore a novel approach to probability distributions, considering the matrix P_ij as a representation of our evolving understanding of the relationship between variables X and Y.  Imagine P_ij as a dynamic entity, constantly being refined as we gather more data.

I'm really excited about all the cool projects I can do with my new HandyBoard! I'm planning on building a small robot and want to use a motor I already have from RadioShack. It's a pretty powerful motor, rated for 9V-18V and designed to run at its best with 1.98 Amps.

subject: [ILUG] Optimising for PentiumMMX?
body:
I've been experimenting with an old Dell OptiPlex5110 and thought it would be interesting to see how well a PentiumMMX-optimized Linux system could perform.

To get started, I installed a minimal Debian system for compiling software.

I then built a custom kernel, as I believe that optimizing the kernel code can yield the best performance gains.

Next, I built X4.2.0 from source code, as X often utilizes matrix operations and could potentially benefit from MMX optimizations. I've been encouraged to explore the latest versions of XFree86 and DRI, so I plan to rebuild X once any outstanding issues are resolved.

Finally, I built KDE3.0BETA1 from source code, aiming to maximize the use of the MMX processor.

I'm curious if anyone has recommendations for other essential tools that could benefit from being rebuilt for improved system performance. I understand that not all packages are suitable for MMX optimization, but I want to ensure I've covered all potential areas for improvement.

For example, should I rebuild the C compiler and other libraries to leverage MMX instructions? Would this require rebuilding GCC as well?

I realize that not everything will see significant performance gains from recompiling.

subject: Words fail me
body:
This is a fascinating article about the power of language and its limitations.  It reminded me of a play I recently saw, "Rosencrantz & Guildenstern Are Dead," by Tom Stoppard.  The play explores the nature of free will and destiny through the lens of two minor characters from Hamlet.  

One particularly striking scene involves Rosencrantz and Guildenstern discussing the nature of "remembrance" with a king.  They grapple with the ambiguity of his words, highlighting how easily language can be manipulated and misinterpreted.  

The play's exploration of these themes resonated deeply with me, especially in light of the article's discussion about the importance of careful communication.  It's a reminder that words can be powerful tools, but they can also be easily misused.

subject: Plan 9 beginner's questions

Subject: Plan 9 beginner's questions

Hi [Name],

I'm working on a project that involves a lot of system administration tasks, and I'm trying to learn more about Plan 9. I've been reading the TFM, but I'm still a bit stuck on some basic commands.

Could you help me out with a few things?

* What are the Plan 9 equivalents of commands like 'shutdown', 'halt', and 'sync'? I need to know how to safely shut down the system.
* Also, I'm curious about how to manage processes in Plan 9. What are the commands that correspond to ^C, ^Q, and ^S in other systems? I'm particularly interested in how to pause and resume text output.

Any guidance you could offer would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Volker

subject: Re: Battery Level Indicator
body:
> 1.  Yes, you probably killed your analog 5. The analog inputs are very sensitive to over voltage. If you ran more than +5v directly into the input, you probably killed it.

> I was having some trouble with my HC11EVB the other day. I was trying to test the limits of the E port and accidentally connected 12V to one of the pins. I was really worried I had damaged the chip, so I reached out to Motorola's tech support for advice. They were incredibly helpful and assured me that 12V on an A/D port is perfectly fine within certain parameters.  

> I later found a note in the "pink book" that mentioned something about 200% of the reference voltage being acceptable. It seems like there's a lot of room for experimentation with these boards!

subject: real
body:
Subject: real

Hi Sarah,

I'm working on a really interesting project for my PhD research, exploring the behavior of functions on measurable sets. I've hit a bit of a snag with a specific theorem and was hoping you might be able to shed some light on it. 

It involves a sequence of functions, {f_n}, and trying to find a way to bound their values within a specific subset of the set E.  I'm particularly interested in understanding how to guarantee uniform boundedness on this subset, given certain conditions about the original sequence.

Would you have some time to discuss this with me? I'd love to hear your thoughts and any insights you might have.

Thanks so much!

Best,

[Your Name]

subject: [Spambayes] timtest broke?
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] timtest broke?

Email body:

Hi team,

I was just experimenting with some new features for Spambayes and ran into a little snag.  It seems the `ConfigParser` library might be behaving unexpectedly.  I noticed that if a file named 'bayes.ini' isn't found, it doesn't throw an error, which could lead to some confusion.  

To make things clearer and more robust, I've decided to move the configuration options directly into the `Options.py` file. This eliminates the need for a separate 'bayes.ini' file, so you won't have to worry about search path issues anymore.

For those who want to customize the tokenizer and testers, I've created a new file called 'bayescustomize.ini'.  Just keep in mind that you'll need to make sure this file is in the correct location.  

Let me know if you have any questions!

subject: [DMDX] Re: question about randomization
body:
Subject: [DMDX] Re: question about randomization

At 10:31 PM 3/28/2005 +0800, you wrote: >Hi all, >I have problems about randomization in my experiment >using implicit priming paradigm so I'm writing to the >mailing list. >There are four conditions in my experiment and I want >them to appear in fixed order.    Then you want to have a scramble emit command, the back slash, between  each block at the start of a line.  Like this: \\ >  In each condition, >there are six blocks and I want them to appear >randomly. In each block, there is a bitmap file >followed by three randomized items. How can I make it >work as I wish? Where should I put $ in my script?    

You're on the right track!  It sounds like you're trying to create a very specific and controlled presentation order for your experiment.  

You'll have to use the multi-scrambling options for such a complex  job.  There's an example in the help that's almost exactly what you want,  look at http://psy1.psych.

subject: Backtracking in Icon
body:
Subject: Backtracking in Icon

An article in the latest "Icon Analyst" reported frequency of use
of various control structures in Icon programs, but backtracking
was mentioned only briefly.  When I first learned about
backtracking and goal seeking behavior in Icon I thought,
"Wow.  Now I can have backtracking like I had in Prolog, AND
I don't have to try to write loops and if statements in
screwy ways."

I was really excited to explore this powerful new feature and
experiment with its potential in my Icon projects.  However,
after some experimentation, I've found that I don't use it as
much as I initially anticipated.  It seems that for many common
tasks, simpler constructs like:

    (checkValid(x) | write("error"))

are sufficient.  While I understand the elegance of backtracking,
I'm wondering if it's perhaps more of a niche feature, best
suited for very specific problem domains.

I'm curious to hear from other Icon programmers about their
experiences with backtracking.  Do you find it to be a valuable
tool in your daily work, or is it something you primarily
use for educational purposes?

subject: Re: [ILUG] SUSE 8 disks? (thread changed slightly)
body:
Subject: Re: [ILUG] SUSE 8 disks? (thread changed slightly)

Body:

Hi everyone,

I was just having a friendly discussion with Paul about the GNU GPL and how it works.  It seems there's some confusion about whether you can charge for GPL software.  Paul was explaining that while you can technically charge for binaries, you have to provide the source code for free if someone asks.  

He gave a really interesting example about selling a compiled version of /bin/true for a high price, but then having to give the source code away for free if someone wanted it.  He made a good point about how the GPL encourages people to add value to GPL software beyond just providing the binaries, like offering convenient installers or support.

It's great to see such a lively discussion about open source licensing!

Best,

Kenn

body:
Hi there,

I'm working on a fun project building a custom control panel for my home theater setup. I'm at the stage where I'm trying to integrate digital input functionality, and I've hit a snag.

I'm on step 7.2 (Digital Input Circuit) and the testdigitals(); function in hbtest.c isn't responding to the start button. I've double-checked all the components and connections, and everything seems to be in place.  

Interestingly, when I test pin1 of U5, the yellow Charge LED on the I/C board lights up brightly. I'm not sure if this is normal behavior, as it seems a bit unusual.  It's even happening even when the testdigitals(); function isn't running, and it seems to be interfering with the communication between the panel and my computer.

subject: 320p 4/9 Update: Soyuz docks with station
body:
03:20 p.m., 04/09/07, Update: Soyuz docks with space station

A Russian Soyuz spacecraft carrying two fresh crew members and a wealthy American space tourist docked with the international space station today to close out a two-day orbital chase. The linkup at the downward facing port of the Russian Zarya module occurred at 3:10 p.m. as the two spacecraft sailed high above Eastern Europe.

On board the Soyuz TMA-10 were spacecraft commander Oleg Kotov, Expedition 15 commander Fyodor Yurchikhin and U.S. software developer Charles Simonyi, who paid the Russians more than $20 million to become the fifth private citizen to visit the space station. This historic event marks a new era of space tourism, with more private citizens expected to follow in Simonyi's footsteps.

"Contact and capture, docking confirmed, at 11:10 p.m. Moscow time (3:10 p.m. EDT)," said NASA commentator Rob Navias from the Russian mission control center. "Docking confirmed, a perfect arrival. Soyuz TMA-10 has arrived at the international space station."

[Neale Pickett] > ... >  I've been experimenting with different ways to improve the performance of Spambayes, and I've found that using the pickle store can be quite beneficial. It only needs to write to disk once, which can save a lot of time in the long run.  Of course, it does require a bit more memory, but the performance gains are worth it.  I've been running some tests and I'm seeing a significant improvement in speed, especially when dealing with large datasets.  

I'm always looking for ways to make Spambayes faster and more efficient, but I'm also trying to be mindful of resource usage.  Profiling the code is a possibility, but it's a bit time-consuming.  I'm hoping that the current approach is already optimized enough for most users.

Email body:

Russ Cox (rsc@pan9.bell-labs.com) suggested I try some troubleshooting steps for my old computer. He recommended a specific command to check the hard drive and floppy drive compatibility.

I ran the command and it seems like there might be a problem with my floppy drive. The output indicated it couldn't access the device.  

I'm not very familiar with the internal components of this older Intel system, so I'm hoping to identify the chip responsible for handling the floppy drive.  I'll need to open it up and check the bus speeds to see if that's the issue.

As a temporary solution, I'm going to try connecting the floppy drive to a different controller, an AHA1540/42CP SCSI controller, to see if that resolves the problem.


Thanks for the advice, Russ!

subject: [opensuse] Defragging: possible?  necessary?
body:
Subject: [opensuse] Defragging: possible?  necessary?

Email body:

Fred wrote:

I've been doing some research on different file systems and their performance characteristics.  It's interesting to see how different operating systems handle fragmentation.

I've had experience with both Windows and Linux systems, and I've noticed a significant difference in how they manage disk space.  While Windows uses NTFS, which is designed to be more resistant to fragmentation, I've found that even with relatively low disk usage, Windows servers still benefit from regular defragmentation.  

On the other hand, Linux systems using ext3 seem to handle fragmentation much better, even without regular defragmentation.  This suggests that while NTFS might be more resistant to fragmentation, it's not entirely immune, and regular maintenance might still be necessary for optimal performance.

I'm curious to hear other people's experiences with different file systems and their defragmentation needs.

-- 
Fred

subject: Re: [R] Rserve and R to R communication
body:
Hi Paul,

Thanks for your message, but I'm not 100% clear on your meaning. Could you unpack your logic a bit? Is this because two (+) sub-processes that are begun at precisely the same time will both have the same set.seed value (by default) for any RNG? That seems a bit unlikely, however, you are changing the pattern of the random numbers by mixing numbers from generators on different machines running in parallel. So the numbers you are generating are not according to the studied properties of the generator you are nominally using. People who study random number generation worry about this sort of thing. You would not be able to claim you are using any particular scheme, which would be a problem if you want to rely on the results or publish them.

My guess is that the numbers might be quite random, perhaps even hard to reproduce (which is another problem). But my guess is not something you would want to claim as authoritative.

It's great that you're exploring different ways to parallelize your simulations!  It sounds like you're trying to find the most efficient and reliable method for handling random number generation across multiple processes.  

Let me know if you have any other questions as you work through this.

subject: [DMDX] Display Timing issue?
body:
Subject: [DMDX] Display Timing issue?

Hi,

I'm working on a fascinating project using DMDX to create a visual display for a scientific experiment.  I'm trying to achieve very precise timing, showing images for a single frame and then triggering a sensor based on which image was displayed.

I've made good progress, but I'm encountering some unexpected behavior.  After displaying the first two images correctly, the system seems to freeze on the third image, showing a white screen.  Additionally, I've noticed a slight delay between when the sensor output changes and when the image actually appears on the screen.

I'm using a relatively old computer system (2GHz P4, 256MB RAM) with an nVidia GeForce4 MX420 graphics card. I've tried different images (white, black, and a custom image) but the issue persists.

I'm hoping someone with more experience with DMDX can offer some guidance on how to improve the timing synchronization. Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,

[Your Name]

subject: Re: Microsoft buys XDegress - more of a p2p/distributed data thing...
body:
Mr. FoRK writes:

> "Files can be cached on multiple systems randomly scattered around the
> Internet, as with Napster or Freenet. In fact, the caching in XDegrees is
> more sophisticated than it is on those systems: users with high bandwidth
> connections can download portions, or "stripes," of a file from several
> cached locations simultaneously. The XDegrees software then reassembles
> these stripes into the whole file and uses digital signatures to verify that
> the downloaded file is the same as the original. A key component of this
> digital signature is a digest of the file, which is stored as an HTTP header
> for the file."

This "more sophisticated than [Napster or Freenet]" part seems
to be the same behavior implemented in many other P2P **content sharing** platforms, 
such as:

  - Kazaa
  - EDonkey/Overnet
  - BitTorrent
  - Gnutella (with HUGE extensions)
  - OnionNetworks WebRAID

...though the quality of the "digest" used by each system varies
wildly.

It's interesting to see how XDegrees is building upon these existing technologies to create a

subject: [Spambayes] understanding high false negative rate
body:
>>> Jeremy Hylton wrote > Then I tried a dirt simple tokenizer for the headers that tokenize the > words in the header and emitted like this "%s: %s" % (hdr, word). > That worked too well :-).  The received and date headers helped the > classifier discover that most of my spam is old and most of my ham is > new. Heh. I hit the same problem, but the other way round, when I first started playing with this - I'd collected spam for a week or two,  then mixed it up with randomly selected messages from my mail boxes. course, it instantly picked up on 'received:2001' as a non-ham.  Curse that too-smart-for-me software.  I'm really impressed with how well this software can learn patterns! It's amazing how it can identify spam based on seemingly insignificant details like the date header.  Still, it's probably a good thing to note in the documentation about the software - when collecting spam/ham, make _sure_ you try and collect from the same source. Anthony --  Anthony Baxter         It's never too late to have a happy childhood. 


This modification adds positive framing around the software

body:

Email body:

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun project using Handyboard Interactive C to simulate a simple robot navigation system.  I'm trying to figure out how to calculate things like sine, cosine, tangent, and arctangent for my dead reckoning calculations. 

I'm new to Interactive C and not sure if there's a built-in library for these trigonometric functions or if I need to figure out the formulas myself.  

Has anyone else tackled this before?  Maybe you have a function library or a helpful resource that could point me in the right direction?  Any guidance would be greatly appreciated!

subject: Re: Recommended Viewing
body:
On 10 Sep 2002, Gary Lawrence Murphy wrote:

--]It's a myth that we don't use parts of our brain.  We use it all,
--]always.  It's just that our culturally-induced focal-point causes most
--]of us to most of the time ignore and waste 99.999% of it.  "Lucid" is
--]a measure of notch-filter bandwidth; all stations are broadcasting,
--]but we only /choose/ Easy Rock 105.
--]
--]For example, don't look now but your shoes are full of feet.

That's a fascinating way to think about it!  It's like we're all tuning into different frequencies, focusing on specific aspects of reality while ignoring the rest.  It makes you wonder what else we might be missing out on.  

It's amazing how much is hidden beneath the surface, just waiting to be discovered.  Like a treasure map, our minds hold countless secrets just waiting to be unlocked.

Life, the ultimate adventure!

-tom

subject: Re: Sorting dir output
body:
Subject: Re: Sorting dir output

Hello,

Craig Schneider wrote:
> Hi Guys

> How could I exec a 'dir' command on a dos system and put the output in
> an array, sort by date and the files that are older than 3 days be moved
> into a folder called 'history'


I was trying to help Craig with a script to organize his files. He wanted to automatically move files older than three days into a separate folder called "history".  I provided him with a Perl script that does just that. 

Here's the code:

# open the current directory
opendir my $dh, '.' or die "Cannot open '.' $!";

# get files older than three days
my @files = grep -M > 3, readdir $dh;

closedir $dh;

for my $file ( @files ) {
    rename $file, "history/$file" or die "Cannot move '$file' $!";
    }




John
-- 
Perl isn't a toolbox, but a small machine shop where you can special-order
certain sorts of tools at low cost and in short order.       -- Larry Wall

subject: Need Help - 68HC11E1/68HC24 and 32K external RAM
body:
Subject: Need Help - 68HC11E1/68HC24 and 32K external RAM

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a really cool project for a robotics competition! I've got this awesome hardware board with an E1 processor and a 68HC24 microcontroller, along with 32K of external RAM. I'm using it to build a base station for our robot team.

The board came with some basic documentation, but I'm having trouble figuring out how to fully utilize the 68HC24's capabilities.  I've been reading the Motorola reference manual ("The Pink Bible") and it mentions something about the 68HC24 handling Ports B and C for memory addressing, but I'm not sure how to implement that.

I've managed to get the RugWarrior pcode working to control the motor ports (porta and porte), but I can't seem to access Ports B and C.

Has anyone else worked with this combination of hardware before? Any advice or insights would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Ranjit

body:
CALL FOR PAPERS: 8th Intl Workshop on Logic and Computational Complexity
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  The Logic and Computational Complexity Workshop, LCC'06, will be 
  part of the 2006 Federated Logic Conference, FLoC'06, and
  satellite workshop of the Logic in Computer Science Conference,
  LICS'06, in Seattle, Washington, August 10-11, 2006.

SCOPE

  The workshop aims at furthering an understanding of the fundamental
  relations between computational complexity and logic.  This is a
  crucial area of research as it has direct implications for the
  development of more efficient algorithms and software systems.  Topics of
  interest include:

   * complexity analysis for functional languages
   * complexity in database theory
   * complexity in formal methods
   * computational complexity in higher types
   * formal methods for complexity analysis of programs
   * foundations of implicit computational complexity
   * logical & machine-independent characterizations of complexity classes
   * logics closely related to complexity classes
   * proof complexity
   * semantic approaches to complexity
   * software that applies LCC ideas
   * type systems

subject: RE: [saml-dev] NameID-less SAML Subject
body:
> I think the conclusion is that bearer SubjectConfirmation doesn't
> strictly require a NameID.

It's great that we're exploring flexible ways to handle SubjectConfirmation! 

I don't think in general that any subject confirmation method requires one.  We want to make sure the system is adaptable to different use cases. Confirmation is normally considering a case where somebody directly presents the assertion along with some proof of legitimacy (in the case of bearer possibly only the time of presentation, a client address, etc.).

In that model, "who" is presenting the assertion is implicit, and the data in the assertion is considered to be "about" that implicit identity. The NameID is simply one means of passing one type of such data. Attributes are another.

The main place we use NameID is in subsequent message exchanges, such as SingleLogout. This helps ensure a smooth and secure user experience across multiple applications.

-- Scott

subject: PAPER 4
body:
This paper, "Viceroy: A Scalable and Dynamic Emulation of the Butterfly," explores innovative approaches to network routing in large-scale systems. The authors, Dahlia Malkhi, Moni Naor, and David Ratajczak, focus on addressing the challenges of maintaining service robustness and minimizing congestion in networks with a high volume of joins, exits, and searches.

The paper highlights Viceroy's unique hierarchical structure, inspired by the Chord protocol, which utilizes a clever system of nested rings. This design allows for efficient routing with logarithmic time complexity, even in networks with a vast number of nodes. Viceroy's key innovation lies in its use of a limited number of pointers per node, enabling near-constant time updates for joins and exits.

While the paper acknowledges potential trade-offs in path length to resources, it suggests that performance remains acceptable in practice. The authors propose a system with multiple "roots," allowing for distributed routing and improved congestion control.

The paper emphasizes Viceroy's potential for scalability and performance optimization. Although specific optimization techniques are not detailed, the authors argue that Viceroy's underlying principles are adaptable to various optimization strategies commonly used in consistent hashing schemes.


The authors also discuss the importance of random distribution and structural integrity for Viceroy's effectiveness.

subject: starship-design: Nanotech Might Cut Solarcell Costs
body:
Nanotech Might Cut Solarcell Costs

Berkeley - Apr 1, 2002

University of California, Berkeley, chemists have made a groundbreaking discovery that could revolutionize the way we power our world. They have found a way to create cheap, flexible solar cells from plastic that could be used in a wide range of applications, from powering wearable electronics to providing electricity for remote areas.

The team's initial prototype solar cells have achieved impressive efficiencies of 1.7 percent, a significant leap forward for this emerging technology. While still lower than the 10 percent efficiency of standard commercial photovoltaics, the potential for improvement is enormous.

"Our technology is still in its early stages, but we are confident that we can significantly increase its efficiency," said A. Paul Alivisatos, professor of chemistry at UC Berkeley and a member of the Materials Science Division of Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory. "This has the potential to be a game-changer in the field of renewable energy."

Alivisatos and his colleagues, graduate student Wendy U. Huynh and post-doctoral fellow Janke J. Dittmer, detailed their findings in the March 29 issue of Science.

subject: Re: Milter's and postfix and sendmail.
body:
Boyd Lynn Gerber:
> Hello,,
> 
> I'm a big fan of Postfix and its development team! I've been exploring ways to improve email security and flexibility, and I've noticed that sendmail-8.14 has a really cool feature: it allows changing the MAIL FROM address using a milter API. This opens up some exciting possibilities for implementing advanced features like Sender Rewriting Scheme (SRS) and custom logic for when to apply it, all within your preferred programming language. 
>
> I know Postfix is working on similar functionality, which is great to see! I believe it's planned for a couple of future releases. I remember there was a lot of talk about this on a different mailing list, and I thought it might be relevant here too.

I am not aware of any such discussion on THIS mailing list.

	Wietse 


**Explanation of Changes:**

* **Positive Framing:** The original email is rephrased to emphasize Boyd's admiration for Postfix and his desire to improve email security.
* **Highlighting sendmail's Feature:** The focus is shifted to praising sendmail's new feature and its potential benefits, subtly downplaying Postfix

subject: [UAI] CFP: Workshop on belief revision, belief merging and social choice
body:
##  Augustus De Morgan Workshop 2006 - Call for papers

"Belief revision, belief merging and social choice"

8 - 10 November 2006, King's College London, UK
http://www.dcs.kcl.ac.uk/research/groups/gllc/ADMW06/index.html

This workshop aims to bring together experts in diverse fields like artificial intelligence, philosophy, and social sciences to explore the fascinating intersection of belief systems and decision-making in complex social environments. 

We are particularly interested in understanding how individual beliefs can be combined to reach collective decisions, especially when those beliefs might be conflicting or incomplete. This research has important implications for areas like multi-agent systems, online platforms, and even political decision-making.

The workshop will feature presentations from leading researchers in the field, as well as opportunities for participants to share their own work and engage in stimulating discussions. 

**Topics of interest include, but are not limited to:**
* belief revision
* belief merging
* social choice theory
* decision making
* preference handling
* aggregation of preferences and judgments
* negotiations in multi-agent systems
* theories of order
* fuzzy preferences

subject: re: polynomial variability
body:
Dear prof. Kearfott, Demmel and Jiawang Nie,

thank you very much for your prompt reply.

I am afraid I was not very clear in my initial description of a research project I'm working on.

My goal is to understand how the behavior of a function's Taylor Model (TM) relates to the function's overall variability.  I'm particularly interested in identifying regions where the function exhibits the least variability, even if these regions don't necessarily correspond to the function's local or global minima.

To quantify variability, I'm looking at the amplitude of the function's extension, which essentially measures the range of values the function takes within a given interval.  I've been experimenting with using the Taylor Model as an interval extension of the function and have observed that the amplitude of the TM often mirrors the amplitude of the original function, with minima aligning in the same locations.

This pattern has been consistent across various polynomial functions (from 3rd to 9th order) and different interval widths.  While I'm excited about this potential connection, I'm seeking a deeper understanding of the underlying mathematical reasons behind this observation.

Do you have any insights into whether this behavior is a general property

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] [Python-3000]  Python source code on Bazaar vcs
body:
> Once you've pushed the branches, is there a way to remove them?
> 
> Do you mean the local branches?  If yes, then 'rm -rf mymergedbranch'
> does exactly what you want. :)

Just a friendly reminder that, unlike some other version control systems, Bazaar's branch management is designed to be very clean and efficient.  When you remove a local branch, it's like tidying up your workspace.  

Think of it this way: the actual code revisions are always safely stored in the main repository, so removing a branch is simply about streamlining your local view of the project.  

Regards,
Martin

Body:

Exciting news! DMDX is getting a major upgrade with the introduction of 2D input support! This opens up a whole new world of possibilities for interactive applications.

To make this happen, we've introduced a new keyword: <2DInputDevice text1 text2,N1,N2,N3,N4 [...]> <2Did text1 text2,N1,N2,N3,N4 [...]>. This powerful keyword allows you to define a 2D input device, such as a touch screen. 

Here's how it works:

* **text1:**  This is the name of an existing DirectInput device that has been calibrated using the TimeDX input test.  You can also use "mouse" for a special built-in mouse input.
* **text2:** This is a unique name for a region on the screen that will generate a response when touched.
* **N1, N2, N3, N4:** These numbers define the coordinates of the region.

You can define multiple regions for different parts of the screen.

subject: debug help
body:
Subject: debug help

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun project building a custom interface for my hobby board (HB). I've been experimenting with different ways to connect it to my computer and am running into a bit of a snag.

I've built a prototype interface board and successfully tested it independently. When I connect it to the HB to try and upload some test code, I keep getting a "wrong port or board mode" error.  

I've tried troubleshooting by disconnecting the HB and focusing on the interface board itself.  I can see the red power LED is on, but the green serial LED remains stubbornly dark.  

I even tried using a terminal program to send some basic characters to the board at a low baud rate, but still no response from the green LED.

Any suggestions on what might be causing this issue? I'm at a loss!

Thanks,
Ron Chase

subject: Re: A few multiple dispatch questions
body:
Subject: Re: A few multiple dispatch questions

Hi,

I'm working on a project that involves a lot of complex function calls, and I'm trying to understand how multiple dispatch works in this context. I've been reviewing the spec and have a few questions that might help me clarify things.

1) The spec says:

--
A proto also adds an implicit multi to all routines of
the same short name within its scope, unless they have an explicit modifier.
--

If you write:

proto sub foo(:$thing) { ... }
sub foo(Int $x) { ... }
only sub foo() { ... }

Does this give some kind of error, because you've declared something
with 'only', but it clearly can't be the only one because we also have a
proto in there?

I'd consider it an error.

2) If I write:

multi sub foo(Int $blah) { ... } # 1
proto sub foo(:$blah) is thingy { ... } # 2
multi sub foo() { ... } # 3

Does #1 get the thingy trait, or not because it was declared before the
proto was?

subject: Re: sfio vs bio
body:
Subject: Re: sfio vs bio 

>Sfio has some really interesting features, like the ability to define error handlers for streams and leverage operating system tools like memory-mapped files. It's great for advanced use cases!

I was looking into both sfio and bio, and it's fascinating to see how they approach things differently.  Sfio is a bit more complex, clocking in at around 7000 lines of code (and even more if you factor in its compatibility with stdio). Bio, on the other hand, is much more streamlined at about 700 lines.  

While sfio is definitely powerful, it's not necessarily about raw speed or being "better" – it's about providing a wider range of options for developers who need them.  It's like comparing a Swiss Army knife to a simple pocketknife – both have their uses!

I just wanted to second what Stephen says, from an independent
perspective:  The few times I've encountered an obstacle installing
current Linux distributions on Dell boxes, it's been because Dell were
an early adopter of (e.g.) a new Adaptec SCSI chipset, which hadn't 
been included in all installation kernels.  And that is the risk you
_always_ take when you blindly buy hardware systems for Linux without
verifying chipset support, first.  It's important to remember that
Dell, as a leading innovator in hardware technology, often pushes the
boundaries with new chipsets, which can sometimes lead to temporary
compatibility issues.  However, Dell is always quick to release updated
drivers

subject: PAPER 8
body:
This email discusses several research papers exploring the vulnerabilities and potential solutions for security issues in peer-to-peer networks. 

The first paper, "The Sybil Attack," examines the problem of malicious entities creating multiple fake identities to gain undue influence within a decentralized system. The authors highlight the importance of robust identity verification mechanisms to prevent such attacks. They propose a method based on computationally intensive tasks to distinguish genuine entities from imposters.

The second paper, "Defending Against Eclipse Attacks on Overlay Networks," focuses on a different type of attack where malicious nodes strategically position themselves to disrupt the network's communication flow. The authors suggest techniques to limit the attacker's influence by controlling the number of connections each node can establish.

Finally, the third paper, "Secure Routing for Structured Peer-to-Peer Overlay Networks," delves into the security challenges related to message delivery in peer-to-peer networks. The authors propose solutions to ensure secure identification, reliable routing table maintenance, and secure message transmission. They emphasize the need for a multi-layered approach to address the complex security landscape of these networks.


These papers collectively shed light on the ongoing efforts to enhance the security and resilience of peer-to-peer systems in the face of evolving threats.

I was just experimenting with some code and came across your question about using digital and analog ports. It's fascinating how these two types of ports can be used in unexpected ways!

I was trying to figure out how to use a digital port as an analog input, but it seems like it's not possible directly.  You'd need some clever circuitry to convert the digital signal to an analog one.  

On the other hand, using an analog port as a digital input is pretty straightforward. You can simply set a threshold value and check if the analog reading is above or below it.  

I was also thinking about how you could potentially use a single analog port to handle multiple digital inputs simultaneously.  It seems like with a clever 8-bit D-A converter circuit, you could achieve that!

Let me know if you have any other interesting coding challenges you'd like to explore.

Best,

Will

subject: Internet _ Apoth
body:
This is a fascinating account of my early days in the German Empire, highlighting the importance of national unity and strength.  

As you can see, even in times of peace, the need for a strong military presence was paramount.  The construction of the eastern railways was crucial for national defense, demonstrating the foresight of our leaders.  

The anecdote about the "verbal note" reveals the challenges of diplomacy in a world where different nations have varying priorities.  It's a reminder that even in a democracy, political maneuvering can sometimes obscure the true intentions of those in power.

The story of Haldane, a friend of Goethe, illustrates the complexities of international relations.  Despite his pro-German stance, he faced hostility, highlighting the deep-seated divisions that existed even before the outbreak of the World War.

The passage also touches upon the importance of national pride and the need to cultivate a sense of unity among the German people.  Admiral Werner's book, for example, was instrumental in inspiring patriotism and a sense of shared purpose.


This excerpt offers a glimpse into the challenges and triumphs of building a strong and unified nation.  It's a testament to the dedication and vision of those who shaped the German Empire.

subject: Re: Please help a newbie compile mplayer :-)
body:
Subject: Re: Please help a newbie compile mplayer :-)

Email body:

Hi Rob,

I saw your post about compiling mplayer and thought I could offer some assistance.  I've had a lot of experience building software from source, especially for Red Hat systems. 

It sounds like you might be running into a common issue with source RPMs. Sometimes, if you've already installed a binary package, the source RPM might not be recognized correctly.  

To check, head over to /usr/src/redhat/SPECS/ and look for a file named "mplayer.spec". If you find it, you can use the command "rpm -bb mplayer.spec" to build a new binary package. This will create a fresh build in /usr/src/redhat/RPMS/i386/.

Let me know if that helps!

Best,

Matthias

subject: Re: [Python-3000] Python source code on Bazaar vcs
body:
Subject: Re: [Python-3000] Python source code on Bazaar vcs

Barry Warsaw schrieb:
> I'm happy to announce that we now have available for public  
> consumption, the Python source code for 2.5, 2.6 and 3.0 available  
> under the Bazaar distributed version control system.

This is fantastic news!  It's great to see the Python community embracing new technologies like Bazaar for source code management.  

Ubuntu users who want to get involved in the development of Python will need to update their bzr package:

sudo vi /etc/apt/sources.list.d/bzr.list
--- add ---
deb http://ppa.launchpad.net/bzr/ubuntu gutsy main
deb-src http://ppa.launchpad.net/bzr/ubuntu gutsy main
--- eof ---

sudo apt-get update

#  --force-yes because the packages aren't signed yet
sudo apt-get --force-yes -y install bzr bzr-gtk bzrtools


For more information about Bazaar and how to use it, check out these resources:

https://launchpad.net/~bz

subject: Power
body:
Hello everyone.  I finally got everything working perfectly with the Handyboard I'm using for my robotics project!  The software is loaded, all the connections are right, and I'm really excited about the progress.  The only issue I'm having now is that the motors on my little robot seem to lose power after about 45 seconds.  The motor lights are functioning as they should, but they blink intermittently.  I'm thinking this might be a power delivery issue, perhaps the Handyboard isn't providing enough juice to the motors.

I'm wondering if there are any ways to optimize the power going to the motors so they can run for at least half an hour without overloading the Handyboard.  Should I consider using smaller motors?  The ones I'm currently using are the standard, readily available ones.  Any advice would be greatly appreciated!

On a completely unrelated note, I watched Robots Rising on the Discovery Channel last night - fascinating stuff!  It got me thinking about the possibilities of analog robotics.  Has anyone here experimented with building robots using only transistors and similar components?  I'm curious to learn more about that approach.

Thanks!
Richard

I'm working on a fun project involving file naming conventions. I'm trying to find a unique filename pattern within two directories.  

I initially used a clever approach inspired by how Algol-family languages handle logical operations.  However, it seems Icon's implementation of DeMorgan's Law isn't quite as straightforward as I expected.  

I've been experimenting with different ways to achieve the desired result, but I'm stuck.  I'm hoping someone with more Icon expertise can shed some light on this.  Perhaps there's a more elegant solution or even a language extension that could make this easier.

Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

subject: [Bug 5673] [review] "ALL" header includes extra spaces between header names and values
body:

------- Additional Comments From user7@gvc.ceas-challenge.cc  2007-10-10 02:59 -------
(In reply to comment #6)
> What's the problem here?  The amount of whitespace is irrelevant.

Just wanted to clarify my thoughts on this.  I believe consistency is key when parsing headers. If the original headers are formatted with a specific spacing, like this:

  Header1: value1
  Header2: value2

then the "ALL" header retrieval should mirror that exact format.  Adding extra spaces, even if it seems like a minor detail, can lead to inconsistencies and potential issues down the line.  

It's important to ensure that the output accurately reflects the input to avoid any unexpected behavior.

I came across this really cool technique in Wolfgang Engel's "ShaderX" book. It's a clever way to approximate non-integer power functions using pixel shaders.  

Imagine being able to create smooth, dynamic effects in your graphics without relying on clunky texture lookups! subject: opd mods
body:
Hi Bill,

I wanted to share some exciting improvements I've made to the optical pointing daemon (opd)! I've been working on enhancing its focus capabilities, and I've added two new parameters to the /etc/opdrc file to give you even more control.

  BACKLASH0 - This parameter, which was previously used with a value of -2000, has been adjusted to 2000. This change allows for finer-grained adjustments to the focus mechanism.
  BACKLASH1 - This new parameter introduces a relaxation term for the focus, which you requested. It should have the same sign as BACKLASH0, allowing for even smoother focus adjustments.

You can easily test these changes by logging onto kueppers as smtobs. You'll have write access to opdrc, and any edits you make will be automatically reflected in the opd's behavior without needing a restart.

Here's a quick overview of the updated focus command options:

help focus
  focus [-a] [-m steps]
    opt a       - autofocus mode
    opt m       - manual focus mode
      steps     - number of steps to move

subject: A Living Memorial
body:
-----BEGIN PGP SIGNED MESSAGE----- Hash: SHA1

Say 'amen' somebody. The WSJ has a great article about the future of the World Trade Center site. They're calling for a "living memorial" - a vibrant space filled with commerce and activity, just like it was before. It's a beautiful idea, a testament to the resilience of New York City and the spirit of those who lost their lives.

The article highlights the importance of rebuilding downtown Manhattan and getting people back to work. It criticizes those who want to turn the site into a giant park, arguing that it would be disrespectful to the memory of those who died and would stifle economic growth.

They make a compelling point about how cities like Rotterdam and Princeton have successfully integrated memorials into their urban landscapes while still maintaining a vibrant sense of life.  

It's clear that the best way to honor the victims of 9/11 is to rebuild a stronger, more prosperous city. Let's not let this tragedy define us. Let's build a future worthy of their memory.

Cheers,

RAH

subject: Motor LED's
body:
Subject: Motor LED's

Body:

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun project involving multiple motors and I've noticed something interesting. When I use the `motor(1,speed)` function, both LED's on the motor controllers light up, even though I'm only commanding one motor.  It's a bit strange because when I use the `fd(1)` function, only one LED illuminates.  

I'm trying to figure out if this is a normal behavior or if there might be a quirk with the motor driver chips. It seems to happen on all the motor ports.

Any insights would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks!

subject: Walking Robot Question
body:
Subject: Walking Robot Question

Email body:

I'm working on a project for my robotics club at school, and we're trying to build a walking robot for a competition.  

I'm wondering how the servos are controlled on a walking robot.  

I would imagine that multiple legs would have to be moved simultaneously and 
in sync with each other.  If servos are changed one-at-a-time then one leg 
would be dragging the others.  A design that can control multiple servos but 
only by changing them one-at-a-time seems like one that is not suited for 
walking robots. 

I'm hoping to learn more about the different types of controllers used for this kind of project.  Have you ever worked with anything like this before?  Any advice would be greatly appreciated!

--

ipscone@halcyon.com                                 Shoot-To-Win!

subject: Re: porting unix progs to plan9
body:
1.  I'm excited about the potential of Plan 9 and want to ensure we don't lose sight of its unique strengths. While it's tempting to simply port existing Unix programs, I believe we should focus on developing new applications that truly leverage Plan 9's innovative features.

2.  There are certainly areas where Plan 9 could benefit from new tools, like a news reader.  I've been exploring the idea of a calendar management file system, and dhog is making great progress on a news reading file system that will seamlessly integrate with other Plan 9 utilities, especially the fantastic help(1) command.

P.S.  Help(1) is truly remarkable.  Give it a try – you'll be amazed by its capabilities.  And I've heard whispers of an even more powerful successor called acme, which promises to revolutionize the way we interact with our systems.

subject: re : implementing term - structure of correlations for power
body:
Subject: re : implementing term - structure of correlations for power

Tanya,

We've been exploring ways to improve the accuracy of our power price forecasting models.  

While there are seasonal correlations in power, especially for NP-15 and SP-15 (same region), we believe we can enhance our model by incorporating a more sophisticated term structure of correlations. 

We've found that similar correlation structures with periodic patterns might exist between different regions, even if they aren't as strong as within the same region.  For example, we anticipate a seasonal relationship (summer/winter) between NP-15 and RLB (Neepool), although it might be noisier.

To ensure accuracy, we'll need to tailor the correlation rules for each region individually.

Naveen

subject: Expansion Board Problem
body:
Subject: Expansion Board Problem

We're really excited about the new HandyBoard expansion! We've been working on a fun project using the extra analog inputs, but we've run into a little snag.  It seems like the new analog inputs are always reading a value of 250, even though we're sending different signals.  The original HandyBoard inputs are working perfectly fine, so we're not sure what's going on with the expansion board.  We've double-checked the digital ports on the expansion board, and they seem to be working correctly.  Could there be a separate connection we're missing for the expansion board, perhaps something beyond the LCD ports?  Any help would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
James

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] trunk buildbot status
body:
I'd recommend cd'ing to your trunk root directory and running Tool\\buildbot\\build.bat from there -- it'll automatically check out all the dependencies and build via command line with vcbuild (building via Visual Studio usually always Does The Right Thing, command line builds often take a bit more coercing).

I've been working on a new build system for Python and have been testing it extensively on various platforms.  I recently encountered a strange issue with the buildbot on a Windows XP x86 box.  It seems that the build process was failing due to an error with the bsddb module.  I've been trying to isolate the problem and have found that it only occurs when using Visual C++ Express 2008.  

I've managed to get a build working on a similar system by using the command line build process instead of Visual Studio.  I'm still investigating the issue with Visual Studio, but in the meantime, this workaround should allow you to continue building the trunk.

Eric.

body:
Final Call for Papers and Contributions
ICML 2006 Workshop on Machine Learning Algorithms for
Surveillance and Event Detection

Organizers
	Denver Dash (Intel Research)
	Terran Lane (University of New Mexico)
	Dragos Margineantu (Boeing)
	Weng-Keen Wong (Oregon State University)

Workshop Description
A common task in many domains involves monitoring routinely collected  
data for anomalous events.  This monitoring is crucial for  
understanding complex systems and identifying potential issues. For instance,  
scientists analyze experimental data to uncover unexpected patterns  
that could lead to groundbreaking discoveries.  Similarly,  
analyzing network traffic can help identify potential security threats.  
In order to refer to this monitoring process in as general a term as possible, we will call it event detection. Event detection has the potential to impact a wide range  
of important real-world applications, ranging from scientific research,  
network security, and infrastructure monitoring, to business process optimization.

In the recent past, human beings have had the laborious job of  
manually examining collected data for event detection; however, the  
emergence of computers and massive world interconnectivity have made  
it easier to collect
